title: Variables
summary: Declare typed names and control reassignment.
refresher: A C++ variable declaration states its type, name, and optional initial value. `const` prevents later assignment, and scope limits where a name can be used.

blocks.body: A C++ variable declaration states its type, name, and optional initial value. `const` prevents later assignment, and scope limits where a name can be used. Declare typed names and control reassignment.

A C++ variable has a fixed type, a scope, and a lifetime. Scope answers where the name is visible. Lifetime answers how long its stored object exists. Local variables begin their lifetime when execution reaches their declaration and end when their block finishes.

blocks.caption: The string remains constant while the integer count is incremented.
Initialize a local variable before any branch can read it.

blocks.body: If ready is false in the wrong version, result was never given a value and reading it is invalid. In the corrected version, zero provides a defined fallback. Use `const` after the type when reassignment should be rejected by the compiler.

blocks.title: C++ detail to check
blocks.body: Reading an uninitialized local variable gives an indeterminate value and can lead to undefined behavior; initialize state before using it.

blocks.prompt: Which declaration creates a changing integer?
blocks.why: A plain int variable can be reassigned after its initialized declaration.
Compound assignment adds three to the numeric value and stores seven.

blocks.title: Variables checklist
items: Keep scope as narrow as practical.
